petitioner_background: The beneficiary is a nutrition professional from Venezuela seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the INA. He serves as Vice President of Product Development for an Internet syndicator of health and medical related content, and his career includes founding and directing a Clinical Nutrition Research Unit at the University of Carabobo in Venezuela, serving as Chief of the Education Division and Director of the National Institute of Nutrition of Venezuela, and serving as General Director of Health and Nutrition for the Government of Venezuela. He has also authored health articles distributed through Univision.com, Mundo Hispanico, and created the MiDieta Internet weight management program targeting U.S. Hispanic communities.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel referenced the beneficiary's resume listing 24 awards. The petitioner submitted 10 foreign language documents with only an 'extraction translation' containing a brief summary rather than full translations. Key awards claimed included the Jose Maria Bengoa Medal (first class), asserted by counsel to be the 'highest distinction and honor granted to any doctor in the nutrition field in Venezuela,' issued every two years for excellence in nutrition. Additional certificates of recognition were submitted in response to the RFE. The director noted the awards were issued by universities, state government agencies, cities, or societies and associations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the beneficiary's self-serving resume is not evidence of accomplishments listed on it, citing Matter of Treasure Craft of California. The petitioner failed to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires full translations of foreign documents, instead submitting only an extraction translation summary. The record contained no official documentation or media coverage of the Jose Maria Bengoa Medal indicating its prominence, and counsel's assertions do not constitute evidence per Matter of Obaigbena and Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez. The petitioner failed to submit evidence regarding the significance of any of the other awards or that they were nationally or internationally recognized.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of the beneficiary's memberships in 13 professional organizations without full translations of foreign membership documents and without evidence of membership requirements. Counsel asserted that at least two memberships/directorships were extraordinary: the beneficiary was president of the Latin American Nutrition Society and was the first honorary member of the Venezuelan Society for Parenteral and Enteral Nutrition, with counsel claiming membership in the latter is limited to doctors of medicine. Counsel referenced a letter from Dr. Arnold Vera as supporting the significance of these memberships.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the record lacked any official documentation, such as bylaws of the associations, reflecting membership requirements. Dr. Vera did not address the membership requirements for the Latin American Nutrition Society and made no mention of the Venezuelan Society for Parenteral and Enteral Nutrition. The AAO independently obtained membership requirements for fellows of the American College of Nutrition, finding they required a doctoral degree, expertise, specialty board certification, five or more published articles, and endorsement from a current fellow (which could be waived) — concluding these were not outstanding achievements, especially in light of the more exclusive master level within ACN. Without documentation of membership requirements, it could not be determined whether any association required outstanding achievements.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The beneficiary's duties as a university instructor were cited, along with editorial positions referenced by counsel and supported only by the beneficiary's resume. The record also contained evidence that the beneficiary served on the advisory board of the World Review of Nutrition and Dietetics, Volume 72, served as editor for 'Actualization En Nutrition Y Dietetica' and 'La Alimentacion Del Nino Menor de 6 Anos en America Latina,' and chaired a panel discussion at the First National Congress of Science and Technology. Evidence of these positions was found among numerous foreign-language documents relating to conference presentations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concurred with the director that university instructor duties could not meet this criterion, but agreed with counsel that the director failed to consider other evidence. While editorial positions supported only by the resume did not count as self-serving, the AAO found that based on the record as a whole — including the advisory board position for World Review of Nutrition and Dietetics, editorial roles for two publications, and chairing a panel at the First National Congress of Science and Technology — the petitioner established the beneficiary meets this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Multiple reference letters were submitted: Michelle Edmonds (20 years in nutrition) asserted thousands relied on the beneficiary's work; Dr. Benjamin Caballero (Director, Center for Human Nutrition at Johns Hopkins) described the beneficiary's role establishing dietary guidelines for children under five while President of the Latin American Society of Nutrition, influencing Latin American nutrition programs; Dr. Arnold Vera (endocrinologist); Dr. Frederick Trowbridge (President of Trowbridge and Associates) discussed the beneficiary's contribution providing health information for Spanish speakers on the Internet; Dr. Karen Donato (U.S. Surgeon General steering committee on obesity); Dr. Carlos Moisa (obstetrician/gynecologist); unsigned letter from Henrique Salas Romer (former Carabobo governor); signed letter from Bruno Bortesi (former advisor to Carabobo governor); Gustavo Coronel (geologist); the petitioning company president discussing the MiDieta program targeting U.S. Hispanic community with 250,000+ readers. On appeal, Dr. Caballero asserted the beneficiary chaired a 1987 UN University/Cavendes Foundation group developing Latin American dietary 'pyramids'; Dr. Artemis Simopoulos discussed founding of the Center for Nutrition Research at Carabobo; Dr. Benjamin Torun claimed their book is part of the Pan American Health Organization's Expanded Program on Textbooks.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the letters unpersuasive. Some references were not experts in nutrition: Dr. Vera and Dr. Moisa practice different areas of medicine, and Mr. Coronel is not even in a field relating to public health. Dr. Vera's resume showed endocrinology practice with no articles directly related to nutrition. Ms. Edmonds did not claim prior knowledge or identify specific contributions. Dr. Donato failed to provide specifics. The unsigned Romer letter had little evidentiary value. Bortesi's claims about the beneficiary being a reference were unsupported by experts in other states/countries. The 1987 dietary guidelines work predated filing by 15 years and was not persuasive evidence of sustained acclaim, and lacked confirmation from UN University or Cavendes Foundation. The Carabobo Center for Nutrition Research, while a leading role, lacked evidence it served as an example for future centers. The book's inclusion in the PAHO Expanded Program on Textbooks had no information about its significance. Not every nutritionist authoring health articles in general media makes a contribution of major significance. All letters were from the beneficiary's immediate circle of colleagues and collaborators, which cannot by themselves establish the beneficiary is known beyond that circle. Letters from independent experts with prior knowledge of the beneficiary's work are more persuasive.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The beneficiary presented work at numerous conferences and authored news articles on health for Univision.com and Mundo Hispanico. On appeal, Dr. Benjamin Caballero attested that the beneficiary's book 'Feeding Children Under 6 Years of Age in Latin America' is part of the Pan American Health Organization's Expanded Program of Textbooks, is considered a treatise in the field, and is used by scholars and professionals. Dr. Caballero further asserted the beneficiary authored chapters in books used by professionals and as textbooks in Spanish-speaking countries worldwide.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO determined that nutritional articles in general media are not scholarly articles published in peer-reviewed nutritional journals and cannot meet this criterion — those articles were considered instead under contributions. Regarding books and book chapters, the petitioner did not submit evidence supporting Dr. Caballero's assertions: no evidence the beneficiary is widely cited, no publishing company data on book sales, and no course syllabi from multiple universities listing the chapters as required reading. The record did not establish the petitioner's history of published articles is indicative of or consistent with national or international acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner offered conference presentations as evidence under this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that according to the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vii), this criterion relates to visual artists and is not applicable to the beneficiary's field. Conference presentations cannot serve to meet it, and even a large number of conference presentations over a lengthy career is not comparable evidence to the exclusive artistic showcases that might meet this criterion for visual artists.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: According to Dr. Caballero, the beneficiary, in consultation with the U.S. National Institutes of Health, founded and directed a Clinical Nutrition Research Unit (CNRU) at the University of Carabobo in Venezuela — one of eight worldwide. Ricardo Maldonado, Dean of Carabobo University, affirmed the beneficiary was the Chief of the Education Division and Director of the National Institute of Nutrition of Venezuela, and the CNRU at the University of Carabobo. The university subsequently named the CNRU after the beneficiary. The beneficiary also served as General Director of Health and Nutrition for the Government of Venezuela.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted the director failed to consider this criterion, but found the evidence amply demonstrated the beneficiary meets it. The AAO concluded these positions — founding and directing the CNRU (one of eight worldwide, later named after him), Chief of Education Division and Director of the National Institute of Nutrition of Venezuela, and General Director of Health and Nutrition for the Government of Venezuela — are clearly leading roles for institutions with a distinguished reputation.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO found the petitioner met only two of the ten regulatory criteria (judging and leading role), falling short of the required three. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner has distinguished himself in his field to such an extent that he achieved sustained national or international acclaim or is within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence indicated talent but was not persuasive that the petitioner's achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field. The burden of proof remains entirely with the petitioner under Section 291 of the Act, and the petitioner did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
Immediate Bus. Sys., Inc. v. Richard, 645 F. Supp. 355 (N.D. Ga. 1986)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues: The petitioner failed to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) by submitting an 'extraction translation' summary rather than full translations of foreign language documents for awards and membership certificates. The director's initial RFE notice was criticized as not being specific about why initial evidence was deficient. Counsel argued the director should have considered a lesser classification, but the AAO rejected this as a petitioner cannot seek multiple classifications with a single petition/fee. An unsigned letter from Henrique Salas Romer was noted as having little evidentiary value. No fraud or misrepresentation findings.
